phase1.explanation:
There are multiple ways to create threads:

1. Extend Thread class:
• class MyThread extends Thread { public void run() { ... } }
• new MyThread().start()

2. Implement Runnable:
• class MyRunnable implements Runnable { public void run() { ... } }
• new Thread(new MyRunnable()).start()

3. Lambda (Java 8+):
• new Thread(() -> { ... }).start()

Key methods:
• start() — starts thread (calls run())
• run() — code executed by thread
• sleep(ms) — pauses thread
• setName(String) — sets thread name
• setPriority(int) — priority (1-10, default 5)
• setDaemon(boolean) — daemon thread (doesn't prevent JVM exit)
• getState() — returns thread state

Important:
• NEVER call run() directly — use start()!
• Thread scheduling is non-deterministic
• Order of execution is not guaranteed

Daemon threads:
• Background tasks (garbage collection, etc.)
• JVM exits when only daemon threads remain

Thread priorities:
• MIN_PRIORITY = 1, MAX_PRIORITY = 10
• Not guaranteed — depends on OS

phase2.errorMessage:
Thread: main
Thread: main

(No error — but the threads DON'T ACTUALLY RUN IN PARALLEL! They run on the main thread.)
phase2.explanation:
Calling run() directly executes the method on the CURRENT thread, not a new thread!

run() is just a method call like any other. It doesn't create a new thread.

FIX: Use start() to create a new thread:
t.start();  // ✓ Creates new thread

start() does two things:
1. Creates a new thread of execution
2. Calls run() on the new thread

Always use start() for multithreading!

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that:
1. Creates 3 threads using Runnable interface
2. Each thread prints numbers 1-5 with a 500ms delay
3. Each thread has a name: "Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma"
4. Start all 3 threads

Bonus: Use lambda syntax for one of them.
dialogueHints: Each thread: new Thread(() -> { for (int i=1; i<=5; i++) { System.out.println(getName() + ": " + i); sleep(500); } })
Set names with setName() or pass to constructor
phase3.openingDialogue: Create threads — start your parallel journey! 🚀
